stimulus:
Welcome!

In this study, you will play three short video games that were generated by Artificial Intelligence (AI) and complete a short survey after each game.

Please make sure you are in a quiet environment where you can focus for the duration of the study. Some games may use sound, so please ensure that your device's volume is not muted.

Press any key to begin.
Press any key to proceed to the first video game. Should you encounter a game-breaking bug, please report the issue in the Menu tab to the right of the screen.

response:
{"creativity-rating":3,"creativity-free-response":"This game is creative in its core concept; the base idea is interesting, and protecting all the cities offers a genuine challenge.

However, the gameplay loop lacks creativity and quickly becomes extremely repetitive. There is a severe lack of visual indicators regarding the protection status of the villages, and the fog wave itself lacks impact and a real sense of threat.

Honorable mention for the areas that resist the ink—that is a genuinely interesting mechanic.
That being said, the ink action is easily spammable during its cooldown. This leads the player to mindlessly click on a house in a loop just to protect it the exact second it refreshes. Finally, the game desperately needs clear timing indicators for the fog's progression.","originality-rating":2,"originality-free-response":"The game lacks creativity, as this type of gameplay has been seen many times before. It strongly resembles the kind of basic Flash games you could easily stumble upon back in 2005. It is extremely poor in terms of novelty, and I see very few, if any, truly original aspects in this game.","effectiveness-rating":3,"effectiveness-free-response":"The game still had a stimulating aspect to it. As a dedicated gamer, I strongly dislike losing, so losing a village felt highly frustrating to me. This specific sense of challenge and difficulty is very appealing to modern players who are tired of overly easy games. However, many elements need to be overhauled for it to truly work.

In conclusion, actively protecting the zones in real-time was fun and engaging. On the other hand, the poor user interface, the severe lack of visual feedback, and the shallow gameplay loop made the overall experience ultimately unamusing.","authenticity-rating":2,"attention-check":3,"intentionality-rating":2,"authenticity-intentionality-free-response":"The game visibly lacks creativity, and it shows; it is completely devoid of authenticity and passion. It is a real-time village protection simulation, but its execution is extremely basic. As I mentioned previously, I feel like I have played this exact type of game hundreds of times on old Flash platforms, which explains my low rating for authenticity.

Furthermore, it is obvious that the developers put very little attention and personal effort into the player's comfort and overall user experience (QoL), which justifies my low score on intentionality as well. It feels like a low-tier game that desperately needs improvements. While it is not the absolute bottom of the barrel in the gaming industry, it requires significantly more polish, refinement, and genuine heart"}

response:
{"creativity-rating":2,"creativity-free-response":"This title relies heavily on classic Flash-era stealth mechanics, which makes the overall game lack true creativity. I have seen this exact formula numerous times before, both in 2D and 3D formats.

However, if I had to point out one genuinely creative element, it would be the mechanic where staying in absolute darkness for too long results in a failure. Beyond that single aspect, the concept remains very basic.","originality-rating":2,"originality-free-response":"The game is highly unoriginal overall. The segments featuring moving lights that must be avoided and basic switches to activate are severely lacking in originality; it is a very cliché and overused formula.

However, the specific mechanic where the player fails if they stay in the absolute darkness for too long is genuinely original. This is a rare concept in the stealth genre, where players can typically hide safely in the shadows indefinitely.

Ultimately, the game offered very few surprises, with the sole exception being this specific threat of dying from the darkness itself.","effectiveness-rating":3,"effectiveness-free-response":"From my perspective, the game was okay and felt like an improvement over the previous title. It was slightly more stimulating as it utilizes concepts like trajectory optimization and precise timing.

Regarding what didn't work well: the visual boundaries of the darkness are very poorly defined. If even a single pixel of the player's hitbox touches the dark zone, it registers as a failure, which feels unfair and frustrating. Furthermore, the core gameplay is somewhat bland, and the moving lights have patterns that are too simplistic to convey a genuine sense of danger.

However, the final switch mechanic worked quite well. It was effectively guarded by a large light halo. Although the halo's movement was cyclic and predictable, it required strict timing to access the switch without being devoured by the absolute darkness.","attention-check":3,"authenticity-rating":2,"intentionality-rating":2,"authenticity-intentionality-free-response":"Overall, the game feels heavily recycled. In old-school stealth games, there were already countless mechanics exactly like this where the sole objective was to avoid the light and move from point A to point B.

The experience is highly unstimulating because it gives off a very basic 'Flash game' vibe. Consequently, it completely fails to reflect any genuine developer intentionality or a sincere effort to create an appealing game. Due to the overall poor quality and the extreme shallowness of the gameplay loop, it feels lifeless. Ultimately, the only concept genuinely worth keeping and expanding upon is the mechanic of dying if you stay in the darkness for too long"}

response:
{"creativity-rating":3,"creativity-free-response":"In my assessment, this game feels slightly more creative than the previous titles. It introduces more parameters to manage, requires precise navigation to overcome specific obstacles, and utilizes a core concept that is noticeably less common than the formulas seen in the first two tests.

The genuinely creative aspects lie in the ability to control the alignment and density of the units—specifically, the tactical mechanic of commanding the swarm to spread out to avoid hazards, or to form a strict, narrow line to pass through tunnels.

However, where it falls short in terms of creativity is its underlying structure. Ultimately, it remains a standard escort game where you move units from point A to point B while dodging environmental elements. It is a solid execution of those mechanics, but it is not revolutionary","originality-rating":3,"originality-free-response":"This game is fairly original, and in my opinion, it features the least common concept among the three games I tested. What makes it genuinely original is the management of secondary parameters within the objectives—specifically, the ability to make the units separate, align, and coordinate.

However, the core concept remains very basic. Aesthetically, it still heavily resembles a low-quality Flash game, which makes it visually poor. Narratively, the swarm mechanic could evoke the idea of migrating birds; while this is a nice concept, it severely lacks immersion, and the overall theme feels undercooked.

Mechanically is where the game is the most interesting, thanks to the different formation options. But there is a massive flaw: the extreme sluggishness. It would make much more sense to significantly increase the units' movement speed and provide larger maps. As it currently stands, the player literally falls asleep before even finishing a single level.","effectiveness-rating":2,"effectiveness-free-response":"Ironically, this was the least enjoyable game of the three. While the core ideas are genuinely better than the previous titles, the extreme sluggishness of the pacing makes it highly irritating and soporific. It is impossible to feel engaged or stimulated while playing. Many players would have undoubtedly quit after the very first stage, if not earlier.

Furthermore, there is a highly frustrating visual issue in the second stage when the player must split the swarm to avoid the dark mass. The mechanic feels extremely unintuitive and poorly executed, as it seems inevitable to lose units. Finally, the dark mass hazard itself is very poorly telegraphed and incredibly hard to see","authenticity-rating":3,"attention-check":3,"intentionality-rating":3,"authenticity-intentionality-free-response":"This game visibly demonstrates a bit more effort in its creativity and gameplay loop by attempting to vary the stages and objectives, which is a definite strength compared to the first two titles.

However, the extreme sluggishness, the poor visual clarity, and the unfairness of the second stage make the overall experience quite dreadful and highly unstimulating. This is exactly why I gave average ratings across the board. The game is slightly more original than the previous two, and one can sense a bit more personal effort put into the core mechanics, but ultimately, the execution remains extremely mediocre."}

response: {"ai_aware_question":"No, the fact that these games were made by an AI did not impact my judgment in any way. I would have rated a game developed by a human with the exact same standards. I was as honest and objective as possible. I have a great deal of respect for their AI and what it is capable of achieving, so its involvement did not bias my review.\n\nThere were some genuinely good ideas and some bad ones. My sole requirement was to determine if the game fulfilled its primary function: to entertain and engage me. The fact that it was generated by an AI does not excuse messy interfaces or punishing, unfair level design."}
